projects.title: Unit 7 Project — Equations & Inequalities in Action
projects.desc: Capstone project applying writing and solving equations and inequalities to real situations.

projects.title: Word-to-Equations Sandbox
projects.desc: Model multi-clue word problems as equations before solving them.

projects.title: One-Step Solver Arena
projects.desc: Solve the one-step equations inside larger problem-solving tasks.

projects.title: Neon Inequality Gatekeeper
projects.desc: Write, solve, and graph inequalities to solve real constraints.

title: Equations and Inequalities Problem Solving
contentObjective: I can model and solve real-world problems using equations and inequalities.
languageObjective: I can explain my reasoning using the words model, equation, inequality, and reasonableness.

turnAndTalk.question: Before solving a word problem, how do you decide whether to model it with an equation or an inequality?
turnAndTalk.kernel: An exact total points to an equation; a limit or range points to an inequality.

stems.en: I would model this with a(n) ___ because the problem says ___.
stems.es: Modelaría esto con un(a) ___ porque el problema dice ___.

stems.en: Words like ___ tell me to use ___.
stems.es: Palabras como ___ me dicen que use ___.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Students use signal words ('equals/total' for an equation; 'at least/at most/no more than' for an inequality) to choose the correct model.
turnAndTalk.extend: How would the same situation change if 'spends exactly $50' became 'spends at most $50'? Justify which model fits each version.
extendStems: 'Exactly $50' needs a(n) ___, but 'at most $50' needs a(n) ___ because ___.

turnAndTalk.question: What was your plan to solve this problem, and how did you check that your answer was reasonable?
turnAndTalk.kernel: I model the problem, solve it, then check the answer against the situation.

stems.en: My plan was to ___, and I knew to ___ because ___.
stems.es: Mi plan fue ___, y supe ___ porque ___.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Students describe modeling the situation, solving with inverse operations, and judging reasonableness by checking the answer fits the real context.
turnAndTalk.extend: Suppose your solution is a negative number or a fraction that doesn't fit the situation. What should you do, and why does reasonableness matter?
extendStems: If my answer doesn't fit, I should ___ because ___.

turnAndTalk.question: Describe a real situation that an equation or inequality could model, and explain which one you would use.
turnAndTalk.kernel: Real situations with limits or exact totals can be modeled mathematically.

stems.en: A real situation is ___, which I would model with a(n) ___.
stems.es: Una situación real es ___, que modelaría con un(a) ___.

stems.en: I chose this model because the situation has ___.
stems.es: Elegí este modelo porque la situación tiene ___.

turnAndTalk.listenFor: Students give a realistic context and correctly match it to an equation (exact value) or inequality (range/limit), justifying their choice.
turnAndTalk.extend: Critique: 'Any word problem can be solved with an equation, so inequalities are never needed.' Use an example to argue why inequalities are sometimes the only correct model.
extendStems: An example that cannot use a single equation is ___ because ___.

vocabulary.definition: To show a real-life situation with an equation or inequality.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Mostrar una situación real con una ecuación o desigualdad.
vocabulary.visual: 'Twice a number is 18' → 2n = 18 — the equation models the words
vocabulary.cloze: A drawing, equation, or diagram that represents a situation is a ___.

vocabulary.definition: A math sentence with an equal sign showing both sides are the same.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Una oración matemática con un signo igual que muestra que ambos lados son iguales.
vocabulary.cloze: A math sentence with an equal sign is an ___.

vocabulary.definition: A math sentence that compares two sides with <, >, ≤, or ≥.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Una oración matemática que compara dos lados con <, >, ≤ o ≥.
vocabulary.cloze: A math sentence that compares values with <, >, ≤, or ≥ is an ___.

vocabulary.definition: Checking if your answer makes sense.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Revisar si tu respuesta tiene sentido.
vocabulary.visual: If the problem asks for a number of people and you get x = −3, that is NOT reasonable
vocabulary.cloze: Checking whether an answer makes sense for the problem is checking its ___.

vocabulary.definition: A letter that stands for the unknown amount in a word problem.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Una letra que representa la cantidad desconocida en un problema verbal.
vocabulary.visual: "5 less than a number is 12" becomes x − 5 = 12, where x is the variable.
vocabulary.cloze: A letter that stands for the unknown amount in a problem is a ___.

vocabulary.definition: A limit in a problem that tells what values are allowed.
vocabulary.definitionEs: Un límite en un problema que indica qué valores se permiten.
vocabulary.visual: "You can spend at most $20" is a constraint: cost ≤ 20.
vocabulary.cloze: A limit that tells which values are allowed in a problem is a ___.

launch.narrative: Detective Santos is closing a complex case. She has collected equations and inequalities from different parts of the investigation. One clue says: 'The total value of stolen items divided equally among 4 accomplices gave each person $85.' Another says: 'The lookout earned less than $50.' She must model both clues mathematically and check if the answers are reasonable. Can you help?
launch.contextImage: Detective at a table assembling puzzle pieces, each piece showing an equation or inequality
noticePrompts: Which clue leads to an equation and which leads to an inequality?
What operation does 'divided equally among 4' suggest?
What does 'less than $50' tell you about the inequality symbol?
wonderPrompts: How do you decide when to use an equation versus an inequality?
What would happen if the lookout earned exactly $50 — would the clue still work?

conceptIntro.heading: Should I model this problem with an equation or an inequality?
conceptIntro.intro: To model means to write a real situation as math. I use an equation when there is one exact total, and an inequality when there is a limit or a range of allowed values.
conceptIntro.keyIdea: 'Equals' or 'exactly' points to an equation (=); 'at least, at most, more than, fewer than' points to an inequality.

iDo.title: Watch me
lines: Clue: 'A total divided equally among 4 people gave each $85.'
This has an exact total, so I use an equation. Let t be the total.
Divided among 4 to get 85 means t ÷ 4 = 85, so t = 85 × 4 = 340.
Check reasonableness: $340 split 4 ways is $85 each. That makes sense!

weDo.title: Let's try together
lines: Clue: 'The lookout earned less than $50.' Equation or inequality?
'Less than' is a limit, so it is an inequality. Let e be the earnings.
'Less than 50' means e < 50. Is $30 a reasonable value? Yes.

youDo.title: Now it's your turn
lines: In the table, decide equation or inequality, write the model, solve, and check it is reasonable.
Then work through the practice problems on your own.

explore.instructions: Read each word problem. Decide if it needs an equation or an inequality. Write the mathematical model, solve it, and check your answer for reasonableness.

rows.wordProblem: A detective splits 42 clues equally among 6 case boards. How many clues per board?
rows.reasonable: Yes — 7 clues per board makes sense.

rows.wordProblem: A witness says the suspect carried fewer than 10 items. If the suspect already had 3, how many more could they carry?
rows.reasonable: Yes — up to 6 more items is reasonable.

rows.wordProblem: The evidence room needs at least 50 storage bins. They already have 28. How many more do they need?
rows.reasonable: Yes — at least 22 more bins makes sense.

hints: If the problem uses 'is,' 'equals,' or 'gives exactly,' use an equation (=). If it uses 'at least,' 'at most,' 'more than,' or 'fewer than,' use an inequality.
After solving, ask: Does my answer make sense in the real-world context? Could the number be negative? A fraction of a person?
explore.label: For each word problem, write the model, solve, and check for reasonableness.

discourse.prompt: How do you decide whether a word problem needs an equation or an inequality?
discourse.sentenceFrame: I use an equation when the problem says ___, but I use an inequality when the problem says ___ because ___.

optional.instructions: Room 1: The case board is a mess. Sort each clue into whether it needs an EQUATION (one exact total) or an INEQUALITY (a limit or range).

items.text: The ransom was split equally among 5 suspects, giving each $40.
A number of fingerprints plus 9 equals 21.
The getaway car held no more than 6 bags of loot.
The alarm must stay on for at least 30 minutes.
The witness saw fewer than 4 accomplices.
Three identical safes hold exactly 75 coins in all.

optional.stem: Room 2: The vault keypad clue reads, 'A number of stolen coins plus 6 equals 14.' Solve x + 6 = 14 to find the code x.
optional.explanation: Subtract 6 from both sides: x = 14 − 6 = 8. Check: 8 + 6 = 14 ✓

optional.stem: Room 3: A sign warns, 'The safe holds at most 20 gold bars.' Which inequality models the number of bars x?
optional.explanation: 'At most 20' means 20 or fewer, so x ≤ 20.

optional.stem: Room 4: A locked drawer clue reads, 'Five identical evidence boxes weigh 60 pounds in all.' Solve 5w = 60 for each box's weight w.
optional.explanation: Divide both sides by 5: w = 60 ÷ 5 = 12 pounds. Check: 5 × 12 = 60 ✓

optional.label: Room 5: Match each crime-scene clue to the model the detective should write.

pairs.term: The reward divided among 4 detectives gave each $50.
A number of clues take away 7 leaves exactly 9.
The patrol must last more than 8 hours.
The locker holds at least 25 files.

optional.title: Level 2 Extension — The Final Door: Catch the Detective's Mistake

workedExample.label: Clue
workedExample.work: Solve the keypad inequality 3x ≤ 18 to find how many bars x fit.

optional.correctWork: To undo multiplying x by 3, you divide both sides by 3 (the inverse operation), not multiply. 3x ≤ 18 becomes 3x ÷ 3 ≤ 18 ÷ 3, which gives x ≤ 6. So at most 6 bars fit, not 54.
hints: The variable is multiplied by 3. What is the inverse operation that undoes multiplying by 3?
Divide both sides by 3: 3x ÷ 3 ≤ 18 ÷ 3, so x ≤ 6.

approaching.stem: A museum has some paintings. After adding 12, they have 45. Which equation models this?
approaching.explanation: 'Adding 12' to the original number gives 45: p + 12 = 45. Solve: p = 33.

approaching.stem: A detective needs more than 8 hours to finish the investigation. She has already worked 3 hours. Which inequality represents the additional hours h she needs?
approaching.explanation: 3 hours plus additional hours h must be more than 8: 3 + h > 8. Solve: h > 5.

approaching.stem: Which model is correct for: 'A number divided by 6 equals 7'?
approaching.explanation: Divided by 6 is n / 6. Equals 7 means = 7. So n / 6 = 7.

approaching.stem: A bus can carry at most 48 passengers. There are already 31 on board. Which inequality shows how many more can board?
approaching.explanation: 'At most 48' means the total must be ≤ 48. So 31 + p ≤ 48. Solve: p ≤ 17.

approaching.instructions: Sort each word problem into whether it needs an equation or an inequality.

categories.label: Equation (=)
items: Five boxes weigh exactly 60 pounds total. How much does each weigh?

categories.label: Inequality (<, >, ≤, ≥)
items: A shelf holds at most 30 books. There are already 17. How many more fit?
The temperature must stay above 32 degrees.

hints: 'At most,' 'at least,' 'above,' 'more than,' and 'fewer than' signal an inequality.
approaching.label: Sort each problem: equation or inequality?

rows.wordProblem: Five identical boxes weigh a total of 60 pounds. How much does each box weigh?
rows.reasonable: Yes — 12 pounds per box is reasonable.

rows.wordProblem: A shelf can hold at most 30 books. There are already 17 books. How many more can be added?
rows.reasonable: Yes — up to 13 more books fits.

rows.wordProblem: A baker split 84 cookies equally into bags of 12. How many bags did she use?

onLevel.label: Write a model, solve, and check reasonableness for each problem.

onLevel.stem: A student solved 4n = 52 and got n = 13. Is the answer reasonable if n represents the number of notebooks in a box?
choices: Yes — 13 notebooks per box is reasonable
Yes — but only if n is a fraction
onLevel.explanation: 4 × 13 = 52 ✓. 13 notebooks per box is a reasonable whole number answer.

onLevel.label: Match each situation to its correct model

pairs.situation: A recipe needs exactly 3 cups of flour.
You must read more than 20 pages tonight.
Each team has the same number of players, and there are 36 players on 4 teams.
The pool temperature must be at least 72°F.

onLevel.label: Solve this two-step problem: 3x + 5 = 26
hints: Then divide both sides by 3: x = 7. Check: 3(7) + 5 = 21 + 5 = 26 ✓

extending.title: Find the Problem-Solving Error

workedExample.label: Problem
workedExample.work: A parking lot holds at most 200 cars. There are 134 already. How many more can park?

extending.correctWork: 'At most' means less than or equal to, so the model should be an inequality: 134 + c ≤ 200. Solving: c ≤ 66. Up to 66 more cars can park, not exactly 66.
hints: The problem says 'at most 200.' Does that mean exactly 200 or up to 200?
'At most' requires ≤, not =. The correct model is 134 + c ≤ 200, so c ≤ 66.

extending.prompt: Create two word problems about the same situation: one that requires an equation and one that requires an inequality. Solve both and explain why the models are different.
extending.sentenceFrame: Equation problem: ___. Model: ___. Solution: ___. Inequality problem: ___. Model: ___. Solution: ___. The models are different because ___.

rows.problem: A tank holds at most 500 gallons. It has 320. How many more gallons can be added?
Six friends split a pizza bill equally. Each paid $8. What was the total?
A runner must complete more than 5 laps. She has done 2. How many more does she need?

extending.label: Check each student's model. If wrong, write the correct model and solve.

optionalActivity.name: Detective Review Escape Room
optionalActivity.intro: Detective Santos locked the evidence vault — model and solve each clue to unlock the next room and crack the case.

connect.scenario: A detective has a $500 budget for equipment. She has already spent $320. The inequality 320 + r ≤ 500 represents the remaining amount r she can spend. She also needs exactly 4 flashlights at $15 each, represented by the equation 4f = 60.
connect.promptQuestion: How much more can the detective spend? How much does each flashlight cost? When do you use an equation versus an inequality?
connect.prompt: The detective can spend at most $__ more because r ≤ 500 − ___ = ___. Each flashlight costs $__ because f = 60 ÷ ___ = ___.

exitTicket.stem: A box of donuts has some donuts. After giving away 7, there are fewer than 5 left. Which inequality represents the starting number of donuts d?
exitTicket.explanation: Starting with d donuts and giving away 7 leaves fewer than 5: d − 7 < 5. Solve: d < 12. ✓
